perturbation.original_text: 12.2 EXCEPTING ONLY CLAIMS MADE PURSUANT TO SECTION 12.1, IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, EXEMPLARY, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES OF ANY KIND, INCLUDING ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUES OR LOST SAVINGS, ARISING OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT AND/OR THE PRODUCTS, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT, STRICT LIABILITY OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF THE PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED, KNOWS OR SHOULD KNOW, OR IS OTHERWISE AWARE OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.2 IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, EXEMPLARY, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES OF ANY KIND, INCLUDING ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUES OR LOST SAVINGS, ARISING OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT AND/OR THE PRODUCTS, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT, STRICT LIABILITY OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF THE PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED, KNOWS OR SHOULD KNOW, OR IS OTHERWISE AWARE OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the exception 'EXCEPTING ONLY CLAIMS MADE PURSUANT TO SECTION 12.1' effectively eliminates the indemnity provision outlined in Section 12.1. This clause, as originally written, ensures that the indemnitor is responsible for protecting the indemnitee against losses, damages, claims, liabilities, and expenses arising from certain specified claims, including failure to perform obligations, negligence, and infringement of proprietary rights. By removing this exception, the modified clause broadly excludes liability for all types of damages, undermining the indemnification agreement which offers protection in scenarios such as gross negligence or willful misconduct, which may violate California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 generally invalidates contracts that exempt parties from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law. The original clause had an exception for claims made pursuant to section 12.1. Section 12.1 provides for indemnity. By removing that exception, section 12.2 now seeks to prevent liability for conduct which would be barred by the statute, in that there are no avenues to seek remedy for harms that the law deems should be recompensed.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1668, as it removes an exception for claims made under section 12.1, which included an indemnity provision. The original text allowed for claims arising from indemnity, while the changed text broadly excludes liability for all types of damages. This violates the law because, according to the Law Explanation, it eliminates avenues to seek remedy for harms that the law deems should be recompensed. The scraped snippet of California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts intending to exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury are against public policy, and the modified clause could be interpreted as attempting to do so by removing the indemnity exception.

perturbation.original_text: 15.5 Injunctive Relief. Each Party agrees that money damages for a breach of its obligations under the provisions of this Agreement protecting Confidential Information and those governing Intellectual Property Rights may be an inadequate remedy for the loss suffered by the other Party and the other Party shall have the right to obtain injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction in order to prevent the breach, or further breach as the case may be, of any such obligation, without limiting the other Party's right to pursue any and all remedies provided in such event by law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.5 Injunctive Relief. Each Party agrees that money damages for a breach of its obligations under the provisions of this Agreement protecting Confidential Information and those governing Intellectual Property Rights may be an inadequate remedy for the loss suffered by the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and the other Party shall have the right to obtain injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction in order to prevent the breach, or further breach as the case may be, of any such obligation, without limiting the other Party's right to pursue any and all remedies provided in such event by law or equity', the contract no longer explicitly grants either party the right to seek injunctive relief in cases of breach related to confidential information or intellectual property rights. This could violate California law which permits injunctive relief to prevent ongoing or threatened harm, especially in cases involving intellectual property.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 526 outlines situations where an injunction may be granted. By removing the explicit right to seek injunctive relief, the contract attempts to limit the remedies available to parties, potentially hindering their ability to protect their confidential information and intellectual property rights as allowed under California law.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit right of a party to seek injunctive relief, which the original text provided. California Code of Civil Procedure § 526(a) states that an injunction may be granted when the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, or when some act during litigation would cause irreparable injury, or when pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. By removing the clause granting the right to injunctive relief, the contract could be interpreted as limiting the remedies available, potentially contradicting the availability of injunctive relief under California law as outlined in CCP 526(a).
